---
title: "Introduction to Health Analytics"
subtitle: "Data visualization"
author: ""
date: "Spring 1 2026"
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
---

# Introduction

Data visualization is a key skill for anyone working with data. In this exercise, we will practice making plots using the `ggplot2` package, which is the most widely used R package for data visualization. Most of the material in this exercise comes from this textbook <https://rafalab.dfci.harvard.edu/dsbook-part-1/dataviz/dataviz-principles.html>

As for all exercises, we start by loading the necessary packages. `ggplot2` is part of the `tidyverse` collection of packages, so we load that. We will also use the `dslabs` package which contains several datasets we will use for practice.

```{r, include=FALSE}
packages = (c("tidyverse", "dslabs", "ggthemes", "ggrepel","ggpubr","scales"))

## Now load or install&load all
package.check <- lapply(
  packages,
  FUN = function(x) {
    if (!require(x, character.only = TRUE)) {
      install.packages(x, dependencies = TRUE, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
      library(x, character.only = TRUE)
    }
  }
)

```

To use **ggplot2** you need functions and arguments. These are hard to memorize, so it is useful to have the ggplot2 cheat sheet handy. You can get a copy from Posit's website[^1] or simply perform an internet search for "ggplot2 cheat sheet".

[^1]: <https://posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/>

# Building a scatter plot with ggplot2

We are going to construct a graph using the US murders dataset included in the **dslabs** package. This dataset contains information about the total number of murders and population size for each of the 50 states in the US in 2010. The data frame has 50 rows and 5 columns:

```{r}
murders <- dslabs::murders
```

## Initializing a plot

Initialize a plot using the murders dataset.

```{r}
p <- murders %>% ggplot()
p
```

## Specifying plot type

Add a scatter plot of total murders versus population size in millions, with population in millions on the x-axis and total murders on the y-axis.

```{r}
p + geom_point(aes(x = population/10^6, y = total))
```

## Adding text labels

Add text labels for each marker showing the state abbreviations

```{r}
p +  geom_point(aes(x = population/10^6, y = total)) + geom_text(aes(population/10^6, total, label = abb))
```

## Global vs. local aesthetic mappings

Edit the code for the plot so the x and y variables and the abbreviation label are specified globally.

```{r}
p_global <- murders %>% ggplot(aes(x = population/10^6, y = total, label = abb))
p_global +  geom_point() + geom_text()
```

## Marker size

Change the size of the markers to 3

```{r}
p_global +  geom_point(size = 3) + geom_text()
```

## Scales

Change the scales of both axes to log scale

```{r}
p_global +  geom_point() + geom_text() + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() 
```

## Shifting text labels

Shift the marker labels to the right by 0.05 units using `nudge_x()`. Set the mark

```{r}
p_global +  geom_point() + geom_text(nudge_x=0.05) + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() 
```

## Adding labels and title

Add a chart title "US Gun Murders in 2010". Add axis labels "Populations in millions (log scale)" and "Total number of murders (log scale)"

```{r}
p_global + geom_point(size = 3)  + 	geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010")
```

## Categories as colors

Add color to the points based on the region of the country (Northeast, South, North Central, West)

```{r}
p_global + geom_point(aes(col=region), size = 3) + 	geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010")
```

## Adding a reference line

Calculate the murder rate (number of murders/total population) per million people across the country. Add a line to the graph showing expected number of murders by population

```{r}
r <- sum(murders$total) / sum(murders$population) * 10^6

p_global + geom_abline(intercept = log10(r), slope = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(col=region), size = 3) + 	geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010")
```

Edit the line representing the average murder rate to make it dashed (\`lty'= 2) and dark gray

```{r}
p_global + geom_abline(intercept = log10(r), lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  geom_point(aes(col=region), size = 3) + 	geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010")
```

## Add-on packages

Change the theme to `theme_economist()`

```{r}
p_global + geom_abline(intercept = log10(r), lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  geom_point(aes(col=region), size = 3) + 	geom_text(nudge_x = 0.05) + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010") + 
  theme_economist()
```

Use the `ggrepel' package to`repel' overlapping text labels. Add a layer `scale_color_discrete(name = "Region")` to capitalize the legend title.

```{r}
p_global + geom_abline(intercept = log10(r), lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  geom_point(aes(col=region), size = 3) +  geom_text_repel() + 	
  scale_x_log10() + 	scale_y_log10() +
	xlab("Populations in millions (log scale)") + ylab("Total number of murders (log scale)") +
	ggtitle("US Gun Murders in 2010") + 
  scale_color_discrete(name = "Region") +
  theme_economist()
```

# Other Geometries

`ggplot2` has many other geometries

## Line plots

Let's use another dataset from the DSLabs package called `gapminder`. This dataset contains information about life expectancy, fertility, population and GDP for several countries over time. Let's plot fertility over time for South Korea.

```{r}
gapminder <- dslabs::gapminder
head(gapminder)
```

```{r}
gapminder %>% filter(country == "South Korea" & !is.na(fertility)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(year,fertility)) +
  geom_line()
```

```{r}
countries <- c("South Korea","Germany")
gapminder |> filter(country %in% countries & !is.na(fertility)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(year,fertility, col = country)) +
  geom_line()
```

## Barplots

`geom_bar` geometry shows the number of observations in each category.

```{r barplot-geom}
murders %>% ggplot(aes(region)) + geom_bar()
```

`geom_col` draws bars with heights that come directly from values you have already computed in your data, rather than counting observations for you. For example, compute the proportion of states in each region and plot this using `geom_col`

```{r}
tab <- murders %>%
  count(region) %>% 
  mutate(proportion = n/sum(n)) 
tab %>% ggplot(aes(region, proportion)) + geom_col()
```

## Histograms

To generate histograms we use `geom_histogram`. Let's plot a histogram of total murders

```{r}
murders %>%
  ggplot(aes(total)) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 20 ,fill = "blue", col = "black")
```

Note that we use the optional arguments `bandwidth = 20` to change the bin size. The default is to create 30 bins. We also use the optional arguments `fill = "blue"` and `col = "black"` to fill the bars with colors and use a different color to outline the bars.

```{r}
bins_5 <- murders %>% ggplot(aes(total)) + geom_histogram(bins = 5)
bins_10 <- murders %>% ggplot(aes(total)) + geom_histogram(bins = 10)
bins_20 <- murders %>% ggplot(aes(total)) + geom_histogram(bins = 20)
bins_30 <- murders %>% ggplot(aes(total)) + geom_histogram(bins = 30)

ggarrange(bins_5, bins_10, bins_20, bins_30, labels=c("5 bins", "10 bins", "20 bins", "30 bins"), vjust=14.5, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)
```

## Density plots

To create a smooth density, we use the `geom_density`. To make a smooth density plot with the data previously shown as a histogram we can use this code:

```{r}
murders %>%
  ggplot(aes(total)) + 
  geom_density(adjust = 1)
```

Note that we use the optional argument `fill` to change the color. To change the smoothness of the density, we use the `adjust` argument to multiply the default value by that `adjust`. For example, if we want the bandwidth to be twice as big we use `adjust` equal to 2.

You can superimpose a density plot on a histogram:

```{r}
murders %>%
  ggplot(aes(x= total, y = after_stat(density))) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth=50) +
  geom_density()
```

`geom_histogram()` computes counts and densities. `after_stat(density)` maps the y-axis to that computed density, so the histogram is on the same scale as `geom_density()`.

## Boxplots

The geometry for boxplot is `geom_boxplot`. As discussed, boxplots are useful for comparing distributions. For example, below are the previously shown heights for women, but compared to men. For this geometry, we need arguments `x` as the categories, and `y` as the values.

```{r}
murders %>%
ggplot(aes(x = region, y = total)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Region",
    y = "Total murders"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Violin plots

```{r}
murders %>%
ggplot(aes(x = region, y = total)) +
  geom_violin() +
  labs(
    x = "Region",
    y = "Total murders"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Exercises

## Ordered bar chart

Plot a bar graph of murder rate per million by state. Colour the bars according to region and sort by size.

```{r}
# First calculate murder rates
murders <- murders %>%
  mutate(murder_rate = total / population * 1e6)

# First calculate murder rates per million
murders <- murders %>%
  mutate(murder_rate = total / population * 1e6)

# Bar plot, coloured by region and sorted by murder rate
ggplot(murders, aes(
  x = reorder(state, murder_rate),
  y = murder_rate,
  fill = region
)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x = "State",
    y = "Murders per million",
    fill = "Region"
  ) +
   theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))
```

## Replicating published scatter chart

Save the data "life-expectancy-vs-gdp-per-capita.csv" into your R project directory. Load it into memory and use it to replicate the figure below as closely as possible! Try out using your favourite large language model e.g. ChatGPT or Claude. What did you enter as a prompt? How well did it do?

To load the data into \`df':

```{r}
df <- read.csv("life-expectancy-vs-gdp-per-capita.csv")
```

![](life-expectancy-vs-gdp-per-capita.jpg)

```{r}
# Fetch the data
df <- read.csv("life-expectancy-vs-gdp-per-capita.csv")

# 1) Label the 4 largest countries (by Population) within each Region
df_labels <- df %>%
  group_by(Region) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Population, n = 4, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

# 2) Region colours (tuned to closely match the screenshot palette)
region_cols <- c(
  "North America" = "#E6847A",  # salmon
  "South America" = "#7A3E3B",  # deep brown-red
  "Africa"        = "#9B63B3",  # purple
  "Europe"        = "#4D73B7",  # blue
  "Asia"          = "#2E9C91",  # teal-green
  "Oceania"       = "#77C6D6"   # light teal
)

ggplot(df, aes(
  x = GDPPerCapita,
  y = LifeExpectancy,
  size = Population,
  colour = Region
)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = df_labels,
    aes(label = Country),
    size = 3.0,                 # 3) smaller text labels
    box.padding = 0.2,
    point.padding = 0.1,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    segment.size = 0.25,
    show.legend = FALSE,
    max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_cols, na.translate = FALSE) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(1000, 2000, 5000, 10000, 20000, 50000, 100000),
   labels = label_comma(prefix = "$", accuracy = 1)
) +
  scale_size_area(
    max_size = 10,              # 3) smaller bubbles
    breaks = c(6e8, 1.4e9),
    labels = c("600M", "1.4B")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Life expectancy at birth",
    x = "GDP per capita (international-$ in 2011 prices; plotted on a logarithmic axis)",
    y = NULL,
    size = "Population",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 10), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9), 
    legend.position = "right"
  )
```

## Exploring the relationship between life expectancy, fertility and income

Answer the following questions using the gapminder dataset contained in the `dslabs` package. The snippet for loading it is repeated below.

```{r}
gapminder <- dslabs::gapminder
head(gapminder)
```

1.  Plot life expectancy against log GDP per capita for 1967 and 2007. How did the relationship between GDP and life expectancy change between 1982 and 2012? Try plotting the two year on the same graph using different colours. Now try plotting them side-by-side using faceting.

```{r}
gapminder %>%
  mutate(gdppc = gdp / population) %>%
  filter(
    year %in% c(1967, 2007),
    !is.na(gdppc),
    !is.na(life_expectancy)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(gdppc, life_expectancy, color = factor(year))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::dollar_format(prefix = "$")) +
  labs(
    title = "Life Expectancy vs GDP per Capita",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy",
    color = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

test <- gapminder %>%
  mutate(gdppc = gdp / population) %>%
  filter(
    year %in% c(1982, 2012),
    !is.na(gdppc),
    !is.na(life_expectancy)
  ) 
```

```{r}
gapminder %>%
  mutate(gdppc = gdp / population) %>%
  filter(
    year %in% c(1967, 2007),
    !is.na(gdppc),
    !is.na(life_expectancy)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(gdppc, life_expectancy)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format(prefix = "$")) +
  facet_wrap(~ year, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Life Expectancy vs GDP per Capita",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

2.  What do you notice about the number of countries on the graph for earlier vs later years? Why do you think this is? How might this bias your interpretation of the relationship between GDP and life expectancy?

*Answer:* The number of countries represented in the earlier year is significantly lower than in the later year (2012). This is because 80 countries are missing GDP for 1967. Countries that have missing data are more likely to be low income when they appear in the data later on.

```{r}
gapminder %>%
  filter(year %in% c(1967, 2007), is.na(gdp)) %>%
  distinct(country, year) %>%
  count(year, name = "n_countries_missing_gdp")
```

3.  Make an interesting graph exploring any aspect of this dataset!
